1: YHWH ‘about to take up Elijah into heaven in a whirlwind’ (behaʿălôt YHWH ʾet-ʾēliyyāhû baśśāʿārāh haššāmāyim) — the Hiphil infinitive construct haʿălôt marks divine agency: YHWH is the actor who causes Elijah to go up. This is the third OT translation narrative: Enoch (‘God took him,’ Genesis 5:24; Hebrews 11:5), Moses (whose grave is unknown, Deuteronomy 34:6 — early tradition understood this as a form of divine concealment), and Elijah. Acts 1:9 describes Jesus’ ascension with the same structure of divine initiative: epērthē (was lifted up, divine passive) and nephelē hypelaben auton (a cloud took him from their sight). The parallel is not incidental — Elijah’s translation to heaven is the OT prototype for the ascension of Christ, with the crucial difference that Jesus first accomplished redemption before ascending to the right hand of the Father.
2: Elijah’s instruction ‘Stay here, for YHWH has sent me as far as Bethel’ (šĕḇ-nāʾ pōh kî YHWH šĕlāḥanî ʿaḏ-bêt-ʾēl) is met with Elisha’s covenant oath: ‘As YHWH lives and as you yourself live, I will not leave you’ (ḥay-YHWH wĕḥê-napšĕḵā ʾim-ʾeʿezbĕkā). The oath-formula is the language of Ruth 1:17 — covenantal loyalty that refuses separation regardless of cost. Elisha’s refusal to leave Elijah mirrors the disciples’ following of Jesus: John 6:68 ‘Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life.’ The journey from Gilgal toward the Jordan recapitulates Israel’s founding geography in reverse, as if tracing the steps back to the originary crossing.
3: The sons of the prophets at Bethel confirm what they know: ‘Do you know that today YHWH will take away your master from over you?’ (hăyāḏaʿtā kî hayyôm YHWH lōqēaḥ ʾet-ʾăḏōnêḵā mēʿal rōʾšĕḵā). Elisha says ‘Yes, I know; keep quiet.’ The foreknowledge shared by the prophetic community recalls the disciples who knew that Jesus was going to the cross (Matthew 16:21-22; John 13:1 ‘Jesus knew that his hour had come to depart’). The silence requested — ‘keep quiet’ (hăḏāšû) — is not suppression of truth but the discipline of grief: some knowledge is held rather than spoken.
4: The pattern repeats at Jericho — Elijah says ‘Stay here’; Elisha refuses with the same oath. The three-part structure (Bethel, Jericho, Jordan) creates a procession to the sacred site of originary crossing. Jericho is the city of Joshua’s first conquest — the place where the Jordan-crossing led to the first redemptive military victory. Elijah’s final journey covers the sacred topography of Israel’s entry into the land.
5: Sons of the prophets at Jericho repeat the same word about Elijah’s imminent departure; Elisha answers identically. The prophetic community’s foreknowledge creates a chorus of witnesses — the departure is not hidden but publicly anticipated. Acts 1:10-11 similarly involves two men (angels) in white robes who interpret the ascension to the astonished disciples: ‘Men of Galilee, why do you stand looking into heaven? This Jesus, who was taken up from you into heaven, will come in the same way as you saw him go into heaven.’
6: Third command to stay; third refusal with the covenant oath. The triple structure creates the narrative equivalent of a solemn ritual — Elisha’s fidelity is tested and confirmed three times before the departure. The fifty sons of the prophets stand at a distance to watch. The community witness creates public accountability for what is about to happen; the translation of Elijah will not be a secret or disputed event within the prophetic community.
7: Fifty sons of the prophets stand opposite them at a distance (waḥămiššîm ʾanšê bĕnê hanněḇîʾîm hālĕḵû wayyaʿamḏû minneḡeḏ mērāḥôq). The number fifty recurs throughout the Elijah-Elisha cycle (the captains of 2 Kings 1, the searchers in vv16-17); the prophetic community is a specific, numbered body. The distance they maintain is both practical (making space for what is about to happen) and theological — one does not approach the divine chariot carelessly. Revelation 4:11 and the heavenly liturgy envisage a similar structure: concentric circles of worshipers around the divine throne, each at its appropriate distance.
8: Elijah takes his mantle (ʾaddartô), rolls it, and strikes the water; the waters divide (wayyēḥāṣĕḇû hēnnāh wāhēnnāh) and the two cross on dry ground. The third parting of the Jordan — after Joshua’s entry (Josh 3-4) and this moment — establishes the pattern: the Jordan-crossing is always a commissioning event. Moses crossed the Red Sea to constitute Israel; Joshua crossed the Jordan to inherit the land; Elijah crosses to be taken to heaven; Elisha recrosses to inherit the ministry. The Jordan-crossing is, in each case, the moment of divine appointment. John the Baptist baptizes at the Jordan — and Jesus comes to be baptized there, after which the Spirit descends and the Father speaks (Matthew 3:13-17): the Jordan becomes the site of the final, definitive commissioning of the one who fulfills all that the Jordan-crossings prefigure.
9: ‘Ask what I shall do for you before I am taken from you.’ Elisha’s request: ‘Let a double portion of your spirit be on me’ (yĕhî-nāʾ pî-šnayim bĕrûḥăḵā ʾēlay). The phrase pî-šnayim (lit. ‘mouth of two’) is the inheritance term of Deuteronomy 21:17: the firstborn receives double the share of any other heir. Elisha is asking to be constituted as Elijah’s spiritual firstborn, the primary heir of the prophetic gift. John 3:34 points to the fulfillment: ‘For he whom God has sent utters the words of God, for he gives the Spirit not by measure (ou gar ek metrou didōsin to pneuma).’ The Son does not receive a ‘double portion’ — he receives the Spirit without any measure at all; the Elisha-type is infinitely exceeded in the antitype.
10: ‘You have asked a hard thing (hiqšîtā lišʾôl). If you see me taken from you, it shall be so for you; but if you do not see it, it shall not be so.’ Sight of the departure is the condition of reception — the disciple who witnesses the translation receives the guarantee of the spirit. Acts 1:8-9 employs the same structure: the disciples are gathered as witnesses of the ascension, and it is precisely because they see him taken up that they can receive what he sends from the Father. The vision of the departing Christ is not a consolation prize but a constitutive act: to see him go is to receive the promise of the Spirit.
11: ‘A chariot of fire and horses of fire separated the two of them, and Elijah went up in the whirlwind to heaven’ (reḵeḇ-ʾēš wĕsûsê ʾēš wayyaḇdilû bên šĕnêhem wayyaʿal ʾēliyyāhû baśśāʿārāh haššāmāyim). The chariot of fire is the divine war-chariot (merkāḇāh) made momentarily visible, the vehicle of YHWH’s glory. Acts 1:9: ‘as they were looking on, he was lifted up (epērthē), and a cloud (nephelē) took him out of their sight’ — the cloud is the same divine presence that filled the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34), led Israel in the wilderness (Exodus 13:21), and filled the temple (1 Kings 8:10-11). Revelation 19:11-14 presents the returning Christ on a white horse with the armies of heaven — the eschatological actualization of what the chariot of fire briefly revealed. Elijah’s ascent is the OT prototype of the ascension: both involve divine initiative, visible transport, and the watching community left behind with a commission.
12: Elisha’s cry: ‘My father, my father! The chariots of Israel and its horsemen!’ (ʾāḇî ʾāḇî reḵeḇ yiśrāʾēl ûpārāšāyw) — then silence; Elijah is gone, and he tears his garments in two. This same cry is repeated at Elisha’s own deathbed (2 Kings 13:14) — the prophets are identified as the true military strength of Israel, the divine protection that makes armies irrelevant. The grief of Elisha at Elijah’s departure echoes the grief of the disciples before Jesus’ departure. John 16:6: ‘Because I have said these things to you, sorrow has filled your heart.’ Jesus’ answer in John 16:7 is structurally identical to what happens in 2 Kgs 2: ‘It is to your advantage that I go away, for if I do not go away, the Helper will not come to you.’ Elijah must depart so Elisha can receive the spirit; Jesus must ascend so the Paraclete can be sent.
13: Elisha picks up Elijah’s mantle that has fallen. The mantle (ʾadderet) is the prophetic insignia — Elijah was identified by it (1 Kings 19:19), and now it falls from him at the translation. To take up the mantle is to accept the prophetic succession, the responsibility for the ongoing prophetic mission. Jesus’ commission to the disciples (Matthew 28:18-20) is the NT form of the mantle-passing: ‘All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me — therefore go.’ The disciples receive not a garment but the Spirit himself (Acts 1:8 ‘you will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes upon you’).
14: Elisha returns to the Jordan, strikes the water with Elijah’s mantle, and says: ‘Where is YHWH the God of Elijah?’ (ʾayyēh YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾēliyyāhû) — the waters divide. The question is a prayer of appeal and an expression of faith: YHWH who worked through Elijah must still work; the God of the departed prophet is not absent with the prophet. Acts 2:33: the ascended Christ ‘having received from the Father the promise of the Holy Spirit, has poured out this that you yourselves are seeing and hearing’ — the God of Jesus is not less present after the ascension but more so, in the Spirit poured out. Elisha’s miracle confirms the succession; Pentecost confirms the commission.
15: ‘The spirit of Elijah rests on Elisha’ (nāḥāh rûaḥ ʾēliyyāhû ʿal-ʾĕlîšāʿ) — the sons of the prophets see it and prostrate themselves. The transfer of prophetic spirit is visible to the community through the confirming sign. John 20:22: Jesus ‘breathed on them and said, “Receive the Holy Spirit.”’ — the Spirit-transfer at the resurrection-appearance, like Elisha’s inheritance at the Jordan, is witnessed and confirmed. The Spirit that rested on Elisha was a ‘portion’ of Elijah’s spirit; the Spirit given by the Son is the Spirit of God himself, given without measure.
16: The sons of the prophets propose sending fifty strong men to search for Elijah’s body — ‘Perhaps the Spirit of YHWH has lifted him and cast him on some mountain or into some valley.’ Elisha says ‘Do not send.’ They press him; he yields. The refusal is theologically significant: Elisha knows the translation is genuine — Elijah has been taken to heaven, not relocated to a mountain. The proposal to search for a body that has been taken to heaven is the exact error that Luke 24:5 addresses: ‘Why do you seek the living among the dead?’
17: They send fifty men for three days and do not find Elijah (lōʾ mĕṣāʾuhû). Elisha says: ‘Did I not say to you, “Do not go”?’ The fruitless three-day search for the translated prophet’s body directly prefigures the empty tomb. Luke 24:1-6: the women arrive at the tomb on the third day, find the stone rolled away and the body not there (to sōma tou kyriou Iēsou), and are told by two men in dazzling apparel: ‘Why do you seek the living among the dead? He is not here, but has risen.’ The structural parallel is exact: a genuine bodily departure to heaven leaves behind a community conducting a body-search that finds nothing, because the body is not there.
18: Elisha’s response to the unsuccessful searchers: ‘Did I not tell you not to go?’ The prophet who received the spirit knows what happened; those who did not witness the departure can only search futilely. The disciples who witnessed the resurrection appearances are the analogous community — Paul in 1 Corinthians 15:1-8 lists them as the ground of the resurrection proclamation. The fruitless search has its christological resolution in the empty tomb that confirms rather than contradicts the departure.
19: The men of Jericho report to Elisha that the water is bad (rāʿîm) and the land is causing miscarriage (hāʾāreṣ mĕšakkulet). The city of Joshua’s first victory is afflicted with water that sustains no life. The curse on creation (Genesis 3:17-19 ‘cursed is the ground because of you’) manifests in unfruitful earth and dangerous water; the land mirrors the spiritual condition of those who work it.
20: Elisha asks for a new bowl (sĕpel ḥăḏāšāh) with salt in it. ‘New’ signals that the instrument must be uncontaminated; the salt is the purifying agent. He goes to the spring of the waters — the source, not the symptom. The healing must be at the origin, not the downstream effects. Jesus similarly goes to the root of spiritual disease — not the symptoms of sin (external acts) but the source (the heart, Matthew 15:18-19). The new bowl anticipates the ‘new covenant’ (kainē diathēkē) in which Jesus is the new vessel of the Spirit’s healing work.
21: Elisha throws salt into the spring and declares: ‘Thus says YHWH: I have healed these waters (rippîtî lamayim hāʾēlleh); death shall no longer come from them, nor shall they cause miscarriage.’ The formula ‘Thus says YHWH’ (kōh ʾāmar YHWH) marks the healing as divine speech-act rather than magical technique; the salt is the sign, not the mechanism. Revelation 22:1-2: ‘the river of the water of life, bright as crystal, flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb’ — the eschatological healing of all waters is the antitype of the Jericho spring. The Spirit is the water that heals at the source (John 4:14 ‘the water I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life’).
22: ‘And the waters have been healed to this day’ (wayyērāpĕʾû hammayim ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh kidiḇar ʾĕlîšāʿ ʾăšer dibber) — the prophetic word effects a permanent change; the spring is healed, not merely temporarily improved. The healing is ongoing: the sign is durable. Matthew 5:13: ‘you are the salt of the earth’ — Jesus identifies his disciples as the purifying, healing presence in a world whose springs are bad. The salt in the new bowl carried by the disciples of Christ is the Spirit himself.
23: Elisha goes up to Bethel. On the way, young men (nĕʿārîm qĕṭannîm) come out of the city and mock him: ‘Go up, baldy! Go up, baldy!’ (ʿălēh qērēaḥ ʿălēh qērēaḥ). The verb ʿālāh (go up) is the same root used of Elijah’s ascent (vv1, 11) and of YHWH’s taking Elijah up — the mocking ‘go up’ is contempt for the prophetic office that Elisha has just inherited, directed at the successor of the translated prophet. The mockery of the prophet is the mockery of the prophetic word and, behind it, of YHWH who sent the prophet.
24: Elisha turns and curses them in the name of YHWH (wayyiqallĕlēm bĕšēm YHWH), and two she-bears come out of the woods and maul forty-two of the youths. The number forty-two reappears in Revelation 13:5 as the duration of the beast’s blasphemy — a period of unchecked mockery of God, after which judgment falls. The incident is severe; it does not yield to domestication. The christological reading is not typological comfort but warning: John 5:23 ‘whoever does not honor the Son does not honor the Father who sent him’; Matthew 12:36-37 ‘I tell you, on the day of judgment people will give account for every careless word they speak’. The mocking of those who bear YHWH’s prophetic name is an act of divine dishonor, and divine honor defends its servants.
25: Elisha goes on to Carmel, then returns to Samaria. The geography traces the prophetic mission: from the site of Elijah’s great victory (1 Kgs 18) back to the capital of the northern kingdom. Elisha enters his ministry where Elijah’s ended, in the places of confrontation with Baal-worship. The prophetic succession is not a fresh start but a continuous mission against the same idolatry in the same places. Jesus sends his disciples not to new territories but back to Jerusalem (Acts 1:8 ‘beginning from Jerusalem’) — the place of crucifixion is the first place of resurrection proclamation.
